html:
When Winter Weather Actually Damages an Ark-La-Tex Roof

Winter storms that cause real damage to commercial roofs in Shreveport-Bossier are the exception, not the rule, but when they happen, they tend to be memorable. The region has seen stretches of hard freeze and ice accumulation severe enough to strain roofs, gutters, and rooftop equipment that are built for occasional cold snaps, not sustained ice loading. Buildings here generally aren't designed the way a northern climate roof is, so a heavy ice event finds the weak points fast.

We're a roofing contractor, not a public adjuster. When a winter event damages a roof we're called to, we inspect it, document what the ice or freeze actually did, and put together a scope that reflects the real damage rather than guessing at what a cold snap might have caused, following the same approach we use for any commercial roof insurance claim.

How Ice and Hard Freeze Damage a Flat Commercial Roof

Ice causes damage differently than wind or hail. Instead of a sudden impact, it's the weight and the freeze-thaw cycle that does the work over hours or days. Ice accumulation on parapet walls and rooftop equipment adds sustained weight in areas that aren't always built to carry it, and water that pools and refreezes at drains or scuppers can back up onto the roof field instead of draining away.

  • Ice buildup on parapet walls, gutters, and rooftop equipment
  • Blocked or frozen drains and scuppers causing water to pond and refreeze
  • Cracked pitch pans or split sealant from repeated freeze-thaw movement
  • Split or burst rooftop piping and equipment lines from hard freeze
  • Membrane stress at seams and flashing points from expansion and contraction

A lot of what we find after a winter event isn't damage to the membrane itself but damage to the drainage and rooftop mechanical systems that keep the roof functioning. A drain that froze solid during an extended cold snap can cause ponding damage well after the freeze has passed, which is part of why a post-event inspection matters even if the roof looks fine on the surface once temperatures come back up. Where the drains or scuppers themselves took the damage, our drain and scupper repair service handles that piece directly, and where the ponding has already caused an interior leak, our leak repair team can respond the same visit.

Documenting a Winter Weather Roof Claim

Cold-weather damage claims need the same kind of dated, specific documentation as any other storm event, tied to the actual freeze or ice event rather than general winter wear. We note what we find, when we found it, and how it connects to the specific cold snap, separating anything that looks like pre-existing wear from damage that's clearly tied to the event. If you need that record for your adjuster, our documentation support service builds it as part of the same inspection.

Building Stock That Sees the Most Winter Strain

Manufacturing and warehouse roofs off I-20 often carry more exposed rooftop mechanical equipment and longer runs of drainage piping, both of which are vulnerable during an extended freeze. Older buildings downtown and around the Red River corridor, including some of the adaptive reuse warehouse space now used for film production, tend to have aging drainage systems that weren't built with hard freeze in mind, which is where we look first after a winter event on those properties.

Why an Inspection After the Thaw Still Matters

It's tempting to assume that once temperatures recover and the ice is gone, any damage would already be obvious. That's not always true. Drainage damage in particular can sit quietly for weeks, showing up later as a ponding complaint or an interior leak that seems unrelated to the freeze that actually caused it. An inspection scheduled once the thaw is complete, rather than skipped because the building looks fine on the surface, is what catches that kind of delayed failure while it's still a straightforward repair instead of a bigger interior problem.

Winter Roof Damage Questions We Hear Most

Does Shreveport-Bossier get enough ice or snow to actually damage a roof?

Most winters, no. But the region has had freeze events severe enough to cause real drainage and membrane damage, and buildings here generally aren't built the way a northern-climate roof is, so those events can hit harder than the same conditions would somewhere used to them.

How do we know if our roof was damaged by a freeze event or just old?

We look for damage patterns tied to freeze-thaw movement and drainage failure specifically, and we separate that from general wear that was already there before the cold snap.

Do you document winter weather damage for insurance purposes?

Yes, the same way we document hail or wind damage: dated photos, a written description tied to the specific event, and a scope for what the repair actually requires.

Can frozen or damaged drains cause damage beyond the immediate freeze?

Yes. A drain that stays blocked or frozen can cause ponding that damages membrane and insulation well after the freeze itself has passed, which is one reason a post-event inspection matters even once temperatures recover.

Will you handle our winter damage claim with the insurance company?

No. We document and substantiate what happened to the roof so you and your adjuster have an accurate scope, but filing and negotiating the claim stays between you and your carrier.
